title: Collingswood, New Jersey

infobox.value: 418th of 565 in state 19th of 37 in county[[1]](#cite_note-CensusArea-1)

is a [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_Jersey) "Borough (New Jersey)") in [Camden County](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), located 5 miles (8.0 km) east of [Center City Philadelphia](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia").[[19]](#cite_note-19) As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the borough's population was 14,186,[[9]](#cite_note-Census2020-9)[[10]](#cite_note-LWD2020-10) an increase of 260 (+1.9%) from the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") count of 13,926,[[20]](#cite_note-Census2010-20)[[21]](#cite_note-LWD2010-21) which in turn reflected a decline of 400 (−2.8%) from the 14,326 counted in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

**Collingswood** is a [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_Jersey) "Borough (New Jersey)") in [Camden County](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), located 5 miles (8.0 km) east of [Center City Philadelphia](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia").[[19]](#cite_note-19) As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the borough's population was 14,186,[[9]](#cite_note-Census2020-9)[[10]](#cite_note-LWD2020-10) an increase of 260 (+1.9%) from the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") count of 13,926,[[20]](#cite_note-Census2010-20)[[21]](#cite_note-LWD2010-21) which in turn reflected a decline of 400 (−2.8%) from the 14,326 counted in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Owing in part to its [Quaker](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") history, Collingswood was founded as a [dry town](/wiki/Dry_county "Dry county") where alcohol cannot be sold, however restaurant patrons are permitted to bring their own wine and beer to consume.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) In July 2015, the town introduced an ordinance that allows craft [breweries](/wiki/Breweries "Breweries") to operate in the town but not serve food. The ordinance passed in August 2015.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Stokes-Lee_House_07.jpg/250px-Stokes-Lee_House_07.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stokes-Lee_House_07.jpg)

Stokes-Lees House, on the 600 block of Lees Avenue

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Collings-Knight_Homestead_Collingswood_NJ.jpg/250px-Collings-Knight_Homestead_Collingswood_NJ.jpg)](/wiki/File:Collings-Knight_Homestead_Collingswood_NJ.jpg)

Collings-Knight Homestead

The land in what is present day Collingswood was originally inhabited by [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"). [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") from [England](/wiki/England "England") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") settled along Newton Creek and [Cooper River](/wiki/Cooper_River_(New_Jersey) "Cooper River (New Jersey)") in the late 17th century, establishing what was known as the [Newton Colony](/wiki/Newton_Colony,_New_Jersey "Newton Colony, New Jersey") and eventually [Newton Township](/wiki/Newton_Township,_Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Newton Township, Camden County, New Jersey"). Much of what is now Collingswood was a farm owned by members of the Collings family during the 18th and 19th centuries. Later a section of [Haddon Township](/wiki/Haddon_Township,_New_Jersey "Haddon Township, New Jersey"), Collingswood was incorporated as a borough by an act of the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") on May 22, 1888, based on the results of a referendum held that same day.[[26]](#cite_note-Story-26) That same year, town resident Edward Collings Knight, a wealthy sugar, real estate and railroad magnate, donated the land that became Knight Park. Knight was a descendant of the Collings family for whom the borough is named.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Collingswood has several historic homes including the 1820s-era house of the Collings family, known as the Collings-Knight homestead, which stands at the corner of Browning Road and Collings Avenue, shadowed by the Parkview at Collingswood apartment homes. The Stokes-Lees mansion located in the 600 block of Lees Avenue dates back to 1707,[[30]](#cite_note-30) making it one of the oldest houses in [Camden County](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey"). Sections of [Harleigh Cemetery](/wiki/Harleigh_Cemetery,_Camden "Harleigh Cemetery, Camden"), the location of poet [Walt Whitman](/wiki/Walt_Whitman "Walt Whitman")'s tomb, are in Collingswood.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Thackara_House.JPG/250px-Thackara_House.JPG)](/wiki/File:Thackara_House.JPG)

The Thackara House on Eldridge Avenue, built in 1754, is one of the oldest houses in Collingswood.

Collingswood is located in the [Philadelphia metropolitan area](/wiki/Philadelphia_metropolitan_area "Philadelphia metropolitan area"), 5 miles (8.0 km) east of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") and 35 miles (56 km) southwest of [Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey"), the state capital of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey").

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the borough had a total area of 1.95 square miles (5.04 km2), including 1.83 square miles (4.74 km2) of land and 0.12 square miles (0.30 km2) of water (5.90%).[[1]](#cite_note-CensusArea-1)[[2]](#cite_note-GR1-2)

[Unincorporated communities](/wiki/Local_government_in_New_Jersey#Unincorporated_communities "Local government in New Jersey"), localities and place names located partially or completely within the borough include Knight Park.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Collingswood shares land borders with [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey"), [Cherry Hill](/wiki/Cherry_Hill,_New_Jersey "Cherry Hill, New Jersey"), [Haddon Township](/wiki/Haddon_Township,_New_Jersey "Haddon Township, New Jersey"), [Oaklyn](/wiki/Oaklyn,_New_Jersey "Oaklyn, New Jersey"), [Pennsauken Township](/wiki/Pennsauken_Township,_New_Jersey "Pennsauken Township, New Jersey") and [Woodlynne](/wiki/Woodlynne,_New_Jersey "Woodlynne, New Jersey").[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

The [Cooper River](/wiki/Cooper_River_(New_Jersey) "Cooper River (New Jersey)") serves as the northern border of Collingswood and Newton Creek as the southern border.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

The [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") counted 14,186 residents in Collingswood. The median household income was $90,184, with 9% of residents at or below the poverty line.[[9]](#cite_note-Census2020-9)

94.9% of residents 25 years or older held a high school diploma or higher, and 55% held a bachelor's degree or higher.[[9]](#cite_note-Census2020-9)

78% of the population identified as white, 8.6% identified as Black, and 1.3% identified as Asian. 7.3% of the population identify as of Hispanic origin.[[9]](#cite_note-Census2020-9)

The median home valuation was $380,400.[[9]](#cite_note-Census2020-9)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

The [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") counted 13,926 people, 6,299 households, and 3,345 families in the borough. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 7,639.4 per square mile (2,949.6/km2). There were 6,822 housing units at an average density of 3,742.3 per square mile (1,444.9/km2). The racial makeup was 81.78% (11,388) [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._census) "White (U.S. census)"), 9.11% (1,268) [Black or African American](/wiki/Black_(U.S._census) "Black (U.S. census)"), 0.32% (45) [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._census) "Native American (U.S. census)"), 2.20% (307) [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._census) "Asian (U.S. census)"), 0.01% (2) [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. census)"), 4.01% (559) from [other races](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_census#Race "Race and ethnicity in the United States census"), and 2.56% (357) from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._census) "Hispanic (U.S. census)") of any race were 9.67% (1,347) of the population.[[20]](#cite_note-Census2010-20)

Of the 6,299 households, 22.6% had children under the age of 18; 36.8% were married couples living together; 12.2% had a female householder with no husband present and 46.9% were non-families. Of all households, 37.0% were made up of individuals and 12.2% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.20 and the average family size was 2.96.[[20]](#cite_note-Census2010-20)

About one-fifth of the population (19.4%) were under the age of 18, 8.0% from 18 to 24, 30.9% from 25 to 44, 28.2% from 45 to 64, and 13.5% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 39.0 years. For every 100 females, the population had 91.1 males. For every 100 females ages 18 and older there were 87.2 males.[[20]](#cite_note-Census2010-20)

The Census Bureau's 2006–2010 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") showed that (in 2010 [inflation-adjusted](/wiki/Inflation_adjustment "Inflation adjustment") dollars) [median household income](/wiki/Median_household_income "Median household income") was $58,769 (with a margin of error of +/− $4,635) and the median family income was $74,236 (+/− $8,567). Males had a median income of $54,088 (+/− $5,121) versus $48,816 (+/− $4,244) for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the borough was $34,126 (+/− $2,577). About 10.7% of families and 12.2% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 19.5% of those under age 18 and 7.9% of those age 65 or over.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Collingswood_exterior_72dpi.jpg/250px-Collingswood_exterior_72dpi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Collingswood_exterior_72dpi.jpg)

The [Perkins Center for the Arts](/wiki/Perkins_Center_for_the_Arts "Perkins Center for the Arts")

Collingswood's retail district is anchored by Haddon Avenue, a section of [County Route 561](/wiki/County_Route_561_(New_Jersey) "County Route 561 (New Jersey)") which runs from [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey") to [Haddonfield](/wiki/Haddonfield,_New_Jersey "Haddonfield, New Jersey"). Collingswood's downtown is known primarily for its restaurants, which span a variety of cuisines.

On Saturdays from May to November, the borough hosts a [farmers' market](/wiki/Farmers%27_market "Farmers' market") under the [PATCO](/wiki/PATCO_Speedline "PATCO Speedline") line, featuring local produce, baked goods, and crafts.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Collingswood Porchfest is an annual music event held in September, featuring more than 100 bands playing music around the borough.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Collingswood sponsors a bike share program, a community [greenhouse](/wiki/Greenhouse "Greenhouse"), as well as a composting program. Beginning in 2009, Collingswood hosts a Green Festival to raise awareness of environmental responsibility.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Monthly "2nd Saturdays" have the borough's art galleries, stores and restaurants hosting new exhibitions by local, national, and international painters, sculptors, and photographers. In 2002, the [Moorestown](/wiki/Moorestown,_New_Jersey "Moorestown, New Jersey") based [Perkins Center for the Arts](/wiki/Perkins_Center_for_the_Arts "Perkins Center for the Arts") opened a second location in Collingswood.

The borough is home to two theater companies, the Collingswood Community Theatre[[48]](#cite_note-48) and the Collingswood Shakespeare Company, which perform throughout the year.

The Scottish Rite Auditorium was built in 1930. The auditorium and its ballroom hosts local theatrical productions and has hosted national recording artists [Ben Folds](/wiki/Ben_Folds "Ben Folds") and [The Beach Boys](/wiki/The_Beach_Boys "The Beach Boys").[[49]](#cite_note-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Collingswood_Theater_Collingswood_NJ_0160.jpg/250px-Collingswood_Theater_Collingswood_NJ_0160.jpg)](/wiki/File:Collingswood_Theater_Collingswood_NJ_0160.jpg)

Collingswood Theater, which now houses retail shops and The Factory, a creative work space

The [Rutgers University-Camden](/wiki/Rutgers_University-Camden "Rutgers University-Camden") based [Symphony in C](/wiki/Symphony_in_C_(orchestra) "Symphony in C (orchestra)") orchestra's administrative and box offices are located on Haddon Avenue.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

The annual Collingswood Book Festival[[51]](#cite_note-51) hosts various authors including [Camille Paglia](/wiki/Camille_Paglia "Camille Paglia") and [Matthew Quick](/wiki/Matthew_Quick "Matthew Quick"), whose novel *[The Silver Linings Playbook](/wiki/The_Silver_Linings_Playbook_(novel) "The Silver Linings Playbook (novel)")* is set in the borough (with scenes set in [Oaklyn](/wiki/Oaklyn,_New_Jersey "Oaklyn, New Jersey"), [Voorhees Township](/wiki/Voorhees_Township,_New_Jersey "Voorhees Township, New Jersey") and the city of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia")), though [David O. Russell](/wiki/David_O._Russell "David O. Russell")'s [film adaptation](/wiki/Silver_Linings_Playbook "Silver Linings Playbook") was set in [Delaware County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Delaware_County,_Pennsylvania "Delaware County, Pennsylvania") and Philadelphia.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Art Within Reach is a program that offers handmade items produced by local artists, with all profits going to the artists. With the 2014 season, the shows are held twice each year at The Factory.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Collingswood has a large [LGBTQ](/wiki/LGBTQ "LGBTQ") community and former Mayor Jim Maley was one of a handful of New Jersey mayors to perform midnight [civil union](/wiki/Civil_union "Civil union") ceremonies the day New Jersey's Civil Union law took effect in 2006; in 2013, former Mayor Maley performed a number of same sex marriages at the Scottish Rite ballroom after same-sex marriage was recognized in New Jersey.[[54]](#cite_note-54) In 2004, *[The Philadelphia Inquirer](/wiki/The_Philadelphia_Inquirer "The Philadelphia Inquirer")* wrote about the borough's "fast growing gay and lesbian community," and the statewide [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") advocacy and education organization [Garden State Equality](/wiki/Garden_State_Equality "Garden State Equality") maintained a Southern New Jersey office in Collingswood until 2010.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Cooper_River_Yacht_Club%2C_Collingswood%2C_NJ.jpg/250px-Cooper_River_Yacht_Club%2C_Collingswood%2C_NJ.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cooper_River_Yacht_Club,_Collingswood,_NJ.jpg)

Sailboats at the Cooper River Yacht Club

Knight Park is the home to daytime recreation in the borough. It is located across the street from [Collingswood High School](/wiki/Collingswood_High_School "Collingswood High School") and it hosts the home games of the high school's sports teams, including women's field hockey, lacrosse, baseball and softball.[[56]](#cite_note-56) During the summer months, the borough offers outdoor movies and classical concerts in the park. Since 1979, the borough has held an annual May Fair festival, which takes place during Memorial Day weekend each year.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

Roberts Pool is accessible to all residents in the summer months.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Collingswood is home to the Cooper River Yacht Club, which offers sailing lessons, events and sailing competitions throughout the year.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Government")]

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Local government")]

Collingswood operates under the [Walsh Act](/wiki/Walsh_Act "Walsh Act") form of New Jersey municipal government. The borough is one of 30 municipalities (of the 564) statewide that use the [commission form of government](/wiki/City_commission_government "City commission government").[[60]](#cite_note-60) The governing body is comprised of three commissioners who are elected [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") on a [non-partisan](/wiki/Non-partisan_democracy "Non-partisan democracy") basis in elections held as part of the May municipal election to serve concurrent four-year terms of office. Each commissioner is assigned a specific department to head in addition to their legislative functions. The commissioners select one of their members to serve as mayor.[[6]](#cite_note-DataBook-6)[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62) The Borough of Collingswood has operated under the Walsh Act since 1917.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit), members of Collingswood's Board of Commissioners are
[Mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") Daniela Solano-Ward (Commissioner of Revenue and Finance),
Deputy Mayor Dr. Amy Henderson Riley (Commissioner of Public Affairs and Public Safety) and
M. James "Jim" Maley Jr. (Commissioner of Public Works, Parks and Public Property), all of whom are serving terms of office ending May 1, 2029.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-Camden2025Municipal-66)

Collingswood's previous Board of Commissioners included
Robert Lewandowski ([Commissioner of Revenue and Finance](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Revenue_and_Finance "Commissioner of Revenue and Finance")), and
Morgan Robinson ([Commissioner of Public Works, Parks and Public Property](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Public_Works,_Parks_and_Public_Property "Commissioner of Public Works, Parks and Public Property")), all of whom served until May 15, 2025.[[3]](#cite_note-Commissioners-3)[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-Camden2021Municipal-68) In December 2020, Commissioner Joan Leonard announced that she would retire "in early 2021" after 24 years of service to the borough.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

In September 2011, the borough experienced a "super downgrade" of its credit rating by [Moody's](/wiki/Moody%27s "Moody's"), from A1 to Ba1, as a result of an outstanding multi-million dollar [loan guarantee](/wiki/Loan_guarantee "Loan guarantee") to a property developer.[[70]](#cite_note-70) Moody's Investors Service issued a report in late May 2012 that restored the Borough of Collingswood's credit rating to an investment grade rating of Baa3.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### Federal, state and county representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Federal, state and county representation")]

Collingswood is located in the 1st Congressional District[[72]](#cite_note-PCR2012-72) and is part of New Jersey's 5th state legislative district.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

For the [119th United States Congress](/wiki/119th_United_States_Congress "119th United States Congress"), [New Jersey's 1st congressional district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_1st_congressional_district "New Jersey's 1st congressional district") is represented by [Donald Norcross](/wiki/Donald_Norcross "Donald Norcross") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey")).[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) New Jersey is represented in the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") by [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") [Cory Booker](/wiki/Cory_Booker "Cory Booker") ([Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), term ends 2027) and [Andy Kim](/wiki/Andy_Kim "Andy Kim") ([Moorestown](/wiki/Moorestown,_New_Jersey "Moorestown, New Jersey"), term ends 2031).[[76]](#cite_note-76)

For the [2026–2027 session](/wiki/222nd_New_Jersey_Legislature "222nd New Jersey Legislature"), the [5th legislative district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_5th_legislative_district "New Jersey's 5th legislative district") of the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") is represented in the [State Senate](/wiki/New_Jersey_Senate "New Jersey Senate") by [Nilsa Cruz-Perez](/wiki/Nilsa_Cruz-Perez "Nilsa Cruz-Perez") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), [Barrington](/wiki/Barrington,_New_Jersey "Barrington, New Jersey")) and in the [General Assembly](/wiki/New_Jersey_General_Assembly "New Jersey General Assembly") by [Bill Moen](/wiki/Bill_Moen "Bill Moen") (D, [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey")) and [William Spearman](/wiki/William_Spearman "William Spearman") (D, Camden).[[77]](#cite_note-77)

[Camden County](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey") is governed by a [Board of County Commissioners](/wiki/Board_of_County_Commissioners_(New_Jersey) "Board of County Commissioners (New Jersey)") composed of seven members chosen [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") in partisan elections for three-year terms on a staggered basis by the residents of the county, with either two or three seats up for election each year as part of the November general election. At a reorganization meeting held in January after each election, the newly constituted Board of Commissioners selects one member to serve as Director and another as Deputy Director, each serving a one-year term in that role.[[78]](#cite_note-About-78) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit), Camden County's Commissioners are:
Commissioner Director Louis Cappelli Jr. ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), Collingswood, 2026),[[79]](#cite_note-79)
Commissioner Deputy Director Edward T. McDonnell (D, [Pennsauken Township](/wiki/Pennsauken_Township,_New_Jersey "Pennsauken Township, New Jersey"), 2025),[[80]](#cite_note-80)
Virginia Ruiz Betteridge (D, [Runnemede](/wiki/Runnemede,_New_Jersey "Runnemede, New Jersey"), 2025),[[81]](#cite_note-81)
Almar Dyer (D, Pennsauken Township, 2027),[[82]](#cite_note-82)
Melinda Kane (D, [Cherry Hill](/wiki/Cherry_Hill,_New_Jersey "Cherry Hill, New Jersey"), 2027),[[83]](#cite_note-83)
Jeffrey L. Nash (D, [Winslow Township](/wiki/Winslow_Township,_New_Jersey "Winslow Township, New Jersey"), 2027),[[84]](#cite_note-84) and

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Education")]

The [Collingswood Public Schools](/wiki/Collingswood_Public_Schools "Collingswood Public Schools") serve students in [pre-kindergarten](/wiki/Pre-kindergarten "Pre-kindergarten") through [twelfth grade](/wiki/Twelfth_grade "Twelfth grade").[[108]](#cite_note-108) As of the 2021–22 school year, the district, comprised of nine schools, had an enrollment of 2,264 students and 200.4 classroom teachers (on an [FTE](/wiki/Full-time_equivalent "Full-time equivalent") basis), for a [student–teacher ratio](/wiki/Student%E2%80%93teacher_ratio "Student–teacher ratio") of 11.3:1.[[109]](#cite_note-NCES-109) Schools in the district (with 2021–22 enrollment data from the [National Center for Education Statistics](/wiki/National_Center_for_Education_Statistics "National Center for Education Statistics")[[110]](#cite_note-110)) are
Collingswood Preschool[[111]](#cite_note-111) with 57 students at Penguin and 43 at Oaklyn in PreK,
James A. Garfield Elementary School[[112]](#cite_note-112) with 131 students in grades K-5,
Mark Newbie Elementary School[[113]](#cite_note-113) with 138 students in grades K-5,
Thomas Sharp Elementary School[[114]](#cite_note-114) with 189 students in grades PreK-5,
William P. Tatem Elementary School[[115]](#cite_note-115) with 244 students in grades K-5,
Zane North Elementary School[[116]](#cite_note-116) with 149 students in grades K-5,
Collingswood Middle School[[117]](#cite_note-117) with 526 students in grades 6-8 and
[Collingswood High School](/wiki/Collingswood_High_School "Collingswood High School")[[118]](#cite_note-118) with 777 students in grades 9-12.[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[120]](#cite_note-120)

Students in [ninth](/wiki/Ninth_grade "Ninth grade") through [twelfth grades](/wiki/Twelfth_grade "Twelfth grade") from [Woodlynne](/wiki/Woodlynne,_New_Jersey "Woodlynne, New Jersey") attend Collingswood High School as part of a [sending/receiving relationship](/wiki/Sending/receiving_relationship "Sending/receiving relationship") with the [Woodlynne School District](/wiki/Woodlynne_School_District "Woodlynne School District"). Students in grades [six](/wiki/Sixth_grade "Sixth grade") through 12 from [Oaklyn](/wiki/Oaklyn,_New_Jersey "Oaklyn, New Jersey") attend the middle and high school as part of a sending/receiving relationship with the [Oaklyn Public School District](/wiki/Oaklyn_Public_School_District "Oaklyn Public School District").[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)

Good Shepherd Regional Catholic School was an elementary school that operates under the auspices of the [Roman Catholic Diocese of Camden](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Camden "Roman Catholic Diocese of Camden").[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124) On April 17, 2020, the Diocese of Camden announced that Good Shepherd was one of five Catholic schools in New Jersey which would close permanently at the end of the school year on June 30, 2020.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Collingswood_PATCO_station_in_Collingswood%2C_NJ.jpg/250px-Collingswood_PATCO_station_in_Collingswood%2C_NJ.jpg)](/wiki/File:Collingswood_PATCO_station_in_Collingswood,_NJ.jpg)

[Collingswood station](/wiki/Collingswood_station "Collingswood station") in Collingswood is the [PATCO Speedline](/wiki/PATCO_Speedline "PATCO Speedline") station to [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") and [Lindenwold](/wiki/Lindenwold,_New_Jersey "Lindenwold, New Jersey").

### Public transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Public transportation")]

The [Collingswood](/wiki/Collingswood_(PATCO_station) "Collingswood (PATCO station)") [PATCO Speedline](/wiki/PATCO_Speedline "PATCO Speedline") [Park and Ride](/wiki/Park_and_ride "Park and ride") station links the town to the eastern suburbs, [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey") and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") by elevated rail. The station is grouped in ride price with [Westmont](/wiki/Westmont,_New_Jersey "Westmont, New Jersey") and [Haddonfield](/wiki/Haddonfield_(PATCO_station) "Haddonfield (PATCO station)").[[126]](#cite_note-126)

Collingswood is also served by [NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") local routes [403](/wiki/403_(New_Jersey_bus) "403 (New Jersey bus)"), [450](/wiki/450_(New_Jersey_bus) "450 (New Jersey bus)"), and [451](/wiki/451_(New_Jersey_bus) "451 (New Jersey bus)") to Camden.[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128)

### Roads and highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Roads and highways")]

As of May 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit), the borough had a total of 41.33 miles (66.51 km) of roadways, of which 31.06 miles (49.99 km) were maintained by the municipality, 8.29 miles (13.34 km) by Camden County and 1.98 miles (3.19 km) by the [New Jersey Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_Jersey_Department_of_Transportation "New Jersey Department of Transportation").[[129]](#cite_note-129)

[U.S. Route 30](/wiki/U.S._Route_30_in_New_Jersey "U.S. Route 30 in New Jersey")[[130]](#cite_note-130) and [U.S. Route 130](/wiki/U.S._Route_130 "U.S. Route 130")[[131]](#cite_note-131) are the main highways serving Collingswood. [County Route 561](/wiki/County_Route_561_(New_Jersey) "County Route 561 (New Jersey)") also passes through the borough locally called Haddon Avenue.[[132]](#cite_note-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Collingswood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Media")]

*The Retrospect*, a weekly newspaper founded in 1921 that covers local news in Collingswood and the surrounding suburban communities (with a companion news website), is headquartered on Haddon Avenue.[[134]](#cite_note-134) In addition, NJ Pen, a hyperlocal news site covers Collingswood and the surrounding suburbs, and [Patch Media](/wiki/Patch_Media "Patch Media") has a Collingswood website.[[135]](#cite_note-135) Collingswood is in the Philadelphia media market.
